paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: All of society does that to some extent—people chasing thrills and action sports or flow states or orgasm. A lot of these are basically just trying to get out of your own head, trying to get away from that voice in your head and this overdeveloped sense of self. I do not want my sense of self to continue to develop and become stronger as I get older. I want it to be weaker and more muted so that I can live much more in present everyday reality and appreciate it very much as a child would.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Reading was my first love. I was a latchkey kid. My mom was working multiple jobs and going to school at night. The library was my after-school center—I'd just go straight there until they closed. But I think even by that point I'd already loved books. I think I just always loved to read because I'm actually an antisocial introvert and I was just lost in the world of words and ideas from an early age.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: I think some of it comes from the happy circumstance that when I was young nobody forced me on what to read. There is a tendency among parents and teachers to say oh you should read this but don't read that. The reality is I just read a lot that by today's standards would be considered mental junk food. But eventually you just get to like reading, you run out of the junk food and then you start eating the healthy food. Your tastes kind of graduate.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: I started treating books like I treat blogs. When I go to a blog, I'll skim through lots of articles until I find one that looks really interesting, then I'll read that whole article all the way through. Now I treat books the same way—I'll skim through a large number of books, I'll put them down, I'll jump around back forward middle until I find a part that's interesting. I'll just consume that piece and I won't feel guilty about having to finish the entire book.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: The important thing is to read every day. It doesn't matter what you do. The best workout for you is the one that you're excited enough to do every day. The same way, the best books to read are the ones that you're excited about reading all the time. I probably spend ten times as much money on books as I actually get through. For every two hundred dollars worth of books I buy, I actually end up making it through ten percent.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: My number one priority in life, above my happiness, above my family, above my work, is my own health. It starts with my physical health, and then second is my mental health, and then third is my spiritual health. Because my physical health became my number one priority, I could never say I don't have time. In the morning, I just work out, and however long it takes is how long it takes. I do not start my day until I'm done working out.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Happiness is really a default state. It's what's there when you remove the sense that something is missing in your life. We are highly judgmental survival and replication machines constantly walking around thinking I need this, I need that, trapped in a web of desires. Happiness is that state when nothing is missing. When nothing is missing, your mind shuts down and stops running into the future or running into the past to regret something or to plan something.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Every positive thought essentially holds within a negative thought. If I say I'm happy, that means I was sad at some point. If I say he's attractive, then that means somebody else is unattractive. Happiness to me is not about positive thoughts, it's about the absence of desire, especially the absence of desire for external things. The fewer desires I can have, the more I can accept the current state of things, the less my mind is moving.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: I try to run my brain in debugging mode as much as possible. This morning I'm brushing my teeth and I start thinking forward to the podcast, I started going through this little fantasy where I imagine you asking me a bunch of questions. Then I caught myself. I said, why am I fantasy future planning? Why can't I just stand here and brush my teeth? The reality is that 95 percent of what my brain runs off and tries to do, I don't need to tackle at that exact moment.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: The real breakthrough for me with jealousy was when I realized at a personal fundamental
